title: Proving and applying similar triangles
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 9 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 7 Geometry, Section 7H. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

workedExamples.title: Example 17 — Proving two triangles are similar (part 1)
Example 17 — Proving two triangles are similar (part 2)

questions.topic: identifying the angle common to two overlapping triangles
questions.answer: ∠C
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: forgetting to state the shared/common angle as one of the equal angle pairs when the two triangles overlap
misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

questions.topic: identifying vertically opposite angles
questions.answer: ∠ACB and ∠ECD
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠ACB and ∠ECD

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: missing that two triangles meeting at a crossing point share a pair of vertically opposite angles
misconceptions.reason: whenever two line segments cross, the angles opposite each other at the intersection are always equal - this is a free angle pair in bowtie-shaped diagrams

questions.topic: identifying alternate angles in parallel lines
questions.answer: ∠BAC = ∠DEC and ∠CBA = ∠CDE
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠BAC = ∠DEC and ∠CBA = ∠CDE

questions.topic: identifying the angle common to two triangles
questions.answer: ∠C
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: forgetting to state the shared/common angle as one of the equal angle pairs when the two triangles overlap
misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

questions.topic: identifying corresponding sides in overlapping similar triangles
questions.answer: i  AC   ii  DB
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  AC   ii  DB

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar, giving reasons
questions.answer: ∠AEB = ∠CDB (alternate angles, EA ∥ DC); ∠EAB = ∠DCB (alternate angles, EA ∥ DC); ∠EBA = ∠DBC (vertically opposite); ∴ ΔAEB ||| ΔCDB (AAA)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠AEB = ∠CDB (alternate angles, EA ∥ DC); ∠EAB = ∠DCB (alternate angles, EA ∥ DC); ∠EBA = ∠DBC (vertically opposite); ∴ ΔAEB ||| ΔCDB (AAA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: jumping straight to the similarity statement without stating the angle/side reasons that justify it
misconceptions.reason: a similarity proof needs each equal angle pair (or side ratio) stated with its reason before the concluding similarity statement - the conclusion alone isn't a proof

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: missing that two triangles meeting at a crossing point share a pair of vertically opposite angles
misconceptions.reason: whenever two line segments cross, the angles opposite each other at the intersection are always equal - this is a free angle pair in bowtie-shaped diagrams

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar, giving reasons
questions.answer: ∠BAC = ∠DEC (alternate angles, AB ∥ DE); ∠ABC = ∠EDC (alternate angles, AB ∥ DE); ∠ACB = ∠ECD (vertically opposite); ∴ ΔACB ||| ΔECD (AAA)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠BAC = ∠DEC (alternate angles, AB ∥ DE); ∠ABC = ∠EDC (alternate angles, AB ∥ DE); ∠ACB = ∠ECD (vertically opposite); ∴ ΔACB ||| ΔECD (AAA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: jumping straight to the similarity statement without stating the angle/side reasons that justify it
misconceptions.reason: a similarity proof needs each equal angle pair (or side ratio) stated with its reason before the concluding similarity statement - the conclusion alone isn't a proof

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: missing that two triangles meeting at a crossing point share a pair of vertically opposite angles
misconceptions.reason: whenever two line segments cross, the angles opposite each other at the intersection are always equal - this is a free angle pair in bowtie-shaped diagrams

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar, giving reasons
questions.answer: ∠C is common; ∠CDB = ∠CEA (corresponding angles, AE ∥ BD); ∠CBD = ∠CAE (corresponding angles, AE ∥ BD); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCAE (AAA)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C is common; ∠CDB = ∠CEA (corresponding angles, AE ∥ BD); ∠CBD = ∠CAE (corresponding angles, AE ∥ BD); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCAE (AAA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: forgetting to state the shared/common angle as one of the equal angle pairs when the two triangles overlap
misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: jumping straight to the similarity statement without stating the angle/side reasons that justify it
misconceptions.reason: a similarity proof needs each equal angle pair (or side ratio) stated with its reason before the concluding similarity statement - the conclusion alone isn't a proof

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar, giving reasons
questions.answer: ∠A is common; ∠ABE = ∠ADC (given); ∴ ΔABE ||| ΔADC (AA) — note 2 angles is enough since ∠AEB = ∠ACD by the angle sum of a triangle
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠A is common; ∠ABE = ∠ADC (given); ∴ ΔABE ||| ΔADC (AA) — note 2 angles is enough since ∠AEB = ∠ACD by the angle sum of a triangle

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: forgetting to state the shared/common angle as one of the equal angle pairs when the two triangles overlap
misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar using SAS
questions.answer: ∠C is common; CA/CD = 6/2 = 3; CE/CB = 9/3 = 3; ∴ ΔCDB ||| ΔCAE (SAS)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C is common; CA/CD = 6/2 = 3; CE/CB = 9/3 = 3; ∴ ΔCDB ||| ΔCAE (SAS)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: dividing sides that don't actually correspond to each other across the two triangles
misconceptions.reason: for SAS, the two ratios must use sides from the SAME two triangles that surround the matched angle - mixing up which side belongs to which triangle gives a meaningless ratio

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: forgetting to state the shared/common angle as one of the equal angle pairs when the two triangles overlap
misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar using SAS
questions.answer: ∠D is common; AD/CD = 28/7 = 4; DB/DE = 48/12 = 4; ∴ ΔABD ||| ΔCED (SAS)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠D is common; AD/CD = 28/7 = 4; DB/DE = 48/12 = 4; ∴ ΔABD ||| ΔCED (SAS)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: dividing sides that don't actually correspond to each other across the two triangles
misconceptions.reason: for SAS, the two ratios must use sides from the SAME two triangles that surround the matched angle - mixing up which side belongs to which triangle gives a meaningless ratio

misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar using SAS
questions.answer: ∠DCE = ∠BCA (vertically opposite); EC/AC = 2/5; DC/BC = 3/7.5 = 2/5; ∴ ΔDCE ||| ΔBCA (SAS)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠DCE = ∠BCA (vertically opposite); EC/AC = 2/5; DC/BC = 3/7.5 = 2/5; ∴ ΔDCE ||| ΔBCA (SAS)

misconceptions.reason: for SAS, the two ratios must use sides from the SAME two triangles that surround the matched angle - mixing up which side belongs to which triangle gives a meaningless ratio

misconceptions.reason: whenever two line segments cross, the angles opposite each other at the intersection are always equal - this is a free angle pair in bowtie-shaped diagrams

questions.topic: giving a reason why two triangles (formed by a shadow) are similar
questions.answer: AAA (all angles are the same, since the sun's rays hit the ground at the same angle)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): AAA (all angles are the same, since the sun's rays hit the ground at the same angle)

questions.topic: proving two triangles are similar in an isosceles triangle diagram, finding an extra angle first
questions.answer: ∠ADC = ∠ACD = 80° (base angles of isosceles ΔADC); ∠ACB = 100° (straight angle); ∠CAB = 60° (angle sum of ΔACB); so ∠DAB = 80°; ∠DAC = ∠DBA (given 20°); ∠D is common; ∠ACD = ∠BAD (both 80°); ∴ ΔACD ||| ΔBAD (AAA)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠ADC = ∠ACD = 80° (base angles of isosceles ΔADC); ∠ACB = 100° (straight angle); ∠CAB = 60° (angle sum of ΔACB); so ∠DAB = 80°; ∠DAC = ∠DBA (given 20°); ∠D is common; ∠ACD = ∠BAD (both 80°); ∴ ΔACD ||| ΔBAD (AAA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: jumping straight to the similarity statement without stating the angle/side reasons that justify it
misconceptions.reason: a similarity proof needs each equal angle pair (or side ratio) stated with its reason before the concluding similarity statement - the conclusion alone isn't a proof

questions.topic: proving a right triangle split by an altitude creates two similar triangles
questions.answer: i  ∠B is common; ∠DAB = ∠ACB (given 90°); ∴ ΔABD ||| ΔCBA (AAA).   ii  ∠D is common; ∠DCA = ∠DAB (given 90°); ∴ ΔABD ||| ΔCAD (AAA)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  ∠B is common; ∠DAB = ∠ACB (given 90°); ∴ ΔABD ||| ΔCBA (AAA).   ii  ∠D is common; ∠DCA = ∠DAB (given 90°); ∴ ΔABD ||| ΔCAD (AAA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: forgetting to state the shared/common angle as one of the equal angle pairs when the two triangles overlap
misconceptions.reason: when two triangles share a vertex or overlap, the angle at that shared point is automatically equal in both triangles (it's literally the same angle) - this is often the easiest pair to spot first

questions.topic: using similar triangles to find three unknown lengths in a right triangle split by an altitude
questions.answer: i  BD = 25/3   ii  AC = 4   iii  AB = 20/3
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  BD = 25/3   ii  AC = 4   iii  AB = 20/3

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: dividing sides that don't actually correspond to each other across the two triangles
misconceptions.reason: for SAS, the two ratios must use sides from the SAME two triangles that surround the matched angle - mixing up which side belongs to which triangle gives a meaningless ratio

questions.topic: proving a length relationship using similar triangles (AE = 4AC)
questions.answer: ∠ACB = ∠ECD (vertically opposite); ∠CAB = ∠CED (alternate angles, DE ∥ BA); ∴ ΔABC ||| ΔEDC (AAA); so DC/BC = EC/AC (corresponding sides); 6/2 = EC/AC; ∴ 3AC = CE; as AC + CE = AE, AE = 4AC
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠ACB = ∠ECD (vertically opposite); ∠CAB = ∠CED (alternate angles, DE ∥ BA); ∴ ΔABC ||| ΔEDC (AAA); so DC/BC = EC/AC (corresponding sides); 6/2 = EC/AC; ∴ 3AC = CE; as AC + CE = AE, AE = 4AC

misconceptions.reason: whenever two line segments cross, the angles opposite each other at the intersection are always equal - this is a free angle pair in bowtie-shaped diagrams

misconceptions.reason: for SAS, the two ratios must use sides from the SAME two triangles that surround the matched angle - mixing up which side belongs to which triangle gives a meaningless ratio

questions.topic: proving a length relationship using similar triangles (BC = 1/2 CE)
questions.answer: ∠C is common; ∠DBC = ∠AEC (given); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCEA (AAA); so DB/AE = BC/CE (corresponding sides); 2/4 = BC/CE; ∴ 4BC = 2CE; BC = 1/2 CE
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C is common; ∠DBC = ∠AEC (given); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCEA (AAA); so DB/AE = BC/CE (corresponding sides); 2/4 = BC/CE; ∴ 4BC = 2CE; BC = 1/2 CE

questions.topic: proving a length relationship using similar triangles (CE = 7/5 CD)
questions.answer: ∠C is common; ∠CBD = ∠CAE (corresponding angles, BD ∥ AE); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCAE (AAA); so CB/CA = CD/CE; 5/7 = CD/CE; ∴ 5CE = 7CD; ∴ CE = 7/5 CD
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C is common; ∠CBD = ∠CAE (corresponding angles, BD ∥ AE); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCAE (AAA); so CB/CA = CD/CE; 5/7 = CD/CE; ∴ 5CE = 7CD; ∴ CE = 7/5 CD

questions.topic: proving a length relationship using similar triangles (AB = 3BC)
questions.answer: ∠C is common; ∠CBD = ∠CAE (given 90°); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCAE (AAA); so BD/AE = CB/CA; 2/8 = CB/CA; 1/4 = CB/(CB + AB), which rearranges to give AB = 3BC
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠C is common; ∠CBD = ∠CAE (given 90°); ∴ ΔCBD ||| ΔCAE (AAA); so BD/AE = CB/CA; 2/8 = CB/CA; 1/4 = CB/(CB + AB), which rearranges to give AB = 3BC
